planting.note: Plant bulbs 2 inches deep in fall beneath deciduous trees; the plant grows actively in late winter and spring before the tree canopy leafs out, then goes dormant in summer.

directions: Plant bulbs in autumn in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to partial shade beneath deciduous trees; wild garlic is a spring ephemeral of European and Asian deciduous forests that completes its entire growing cycle from late winter through late spring before going fully dormant. The broad, lance-shaped leaves with a distinctive garlic aroma can be harvested as soon as they emerge in late winter for salads, pesto, and compound butters; they are milder and more versatile than cultivated garlic leaves. White star-shaped flowers in spring are also edible and edible. The plant naturalizes freely by self-seeding to form large carpets — plant densely from the start for a showy spring display under trees.

planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring, spacing them closely (6–8 inches) for faster coverage; this sedge spreads slowly by stolons to fill in over 2–3 years.

directions: Plant divisions or plugs in dry to moist, acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; this fine-textured native sedge is one of the best lawn substitutes for shaded areas under oak trees and other acid-soil trees where grass refuses to grow. It forms a soft, undulating carpet of arching, deep-green foliage that requires little or no mowing — mow once in late winter if desired for tidiness. Unlike most groundcovers, it tolerates both dry shade under large trees and occasional foot traffic. Plant at 6-inch spacing for coverage within 2 years; once established it spreads steadily by stolons without becoming invasive.

planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface in spring (seed needs light to germinate); can also self-sow readily once established to naturalize in a meadow or edge planting.

directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface — lightly pressed in but not covered — in moist, average soil in full sun to partial shade; despite belonging to the iris family, this diminutive native looks like a grass until the small blue-violet flowers with yellow centers open in late spring. It blooms most heavily in spring but continues sporadically through summer, and the tufted grass-like foliage remains attractive all season. Allow some seed heads to ripen for naturalization; the plant self-sows freely in favorable conditions to form charming colonies in meadow edges and lightly shaded areas. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seeds for 60–90 days or direct sow in fall; requires 2–3 years from seed to first bloom. Deep taproot makes transplanting of established plants impossible.

directions: Sow cold-stratified seed in deeply prepared, well-drained soil in full sun and be patient — the compass plant spends its first 2–3 years building a massive taproot that may reach 14 feet deep before producing its first flower stalk. Once established it is virtually drought-proof and may live for decades. The deeply lobed basal leaves align their edges north-south to minimize midday sun exposure, giving the plant its common name and serving as a primitive compass for prairie travelers. The tall, yellow composite flower stalks in midsummer provide pollen for native bees; goldfinches harvest the seeds in fall. Never transplant established plants.

planting.note: Surface-sow seed (do not cover — light required for germination) 4–6 weeks before last frost; alternatively divide rosettes in early spring from established plants.

directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface — do not cover, as light is essential for germination — in consistently moist, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun. This native provides brilliant blue flower spikes in late summer when most other plants are winding down, bridging the gap between midsummer and fall bloomers. It naturally grows in wet meadows, stream banks, and woodland edges and is one of the best plants for rain gardens, retention areas, and moist shade gardens. The plant is monocarpic (dies after flowering) but reliably self-sows in favorable conditions, and new basal rosettes can be separated and replanted in fall or early spring. Excellent companion for cardinal flower (L. cardinalis) in moist areas.
